body.text: The Liber de vita can look like amiscellaneous collection: ahealth manual for scholars, an anti-aging treatise, abook of astrological magic, a Christian apology. The capstone argues that these form one progression. Ficino himself declares the structure in the opening:
"The first concerns healthy life for those devoted to letters. The second concerns long life. The third concerns life obtained from the heavens."

The three books form asingle practical Platonist ascent. Book I: the instrument of knowing is damaged by the act of study and must be repaired. Book II: the instrument must be preserved through time because knowledge requires duration. Book III: the preserved instrument can be opened to the living cosmos through adapted reception. Repair, preservation, opening. These three constitute the ascent. Book Iestablishes the problem that makes the rest necessary. The scholar is not afloating mind but afragile embodied instrument. De vita I.2 — "Painters care for their brushes. Soldiers care for their weapons. Musicians care for their instruments. Yet scholars neglect the instrument by which they measure and grasp the whole world. That instrument is the spirit."

"The soul and body, far different in nature, are joined together by the spirit as amedium, which is acertain very thin and clear vapour, produced by the heat of the heart from the thinnest part of the blood."

Anima et corpus natura longe inter se diversa spiritu medio copulantur, qui vapor quidam est tenuissimus et perlucidus, per cordis calorem ex subtilissima parte sanguinis genitus. The chain: the stomach and liver produce blood. The heart refines blood into spirit. Spirit rises to the brain. The brain serves the senses. The senses serve reason. Study damages this chain. Philosophy half-kills the body. The more the mind succeeds in turning toward incorporeal truth, the more the body is strained beyond its ordinary capacity. De vita I.4 — "Intense mental activity dries the brain, consumes its moisture, weakens natural heat, disperses the spirits, uses up the subtler blood, leaves the remaining blood dense, thick, and dark, pulls nature toward brain and heart, abandons the stomach and liver, impairs digestion, and produces dark vapours that rise to the soul, causing fear, sadness, and disturbed judgment." De vita I.26 closes Book Iwith the hierarchy that prevents medicine from becoming the whole story: "Care for the corporeal spirit. Cultivate the incorporeal. Venerate truth." Medicine is the first stage, not the last. Corporeum quidem spiritum cura; incorporeum vero cole; veritatem denique venerare. Book II argues that knowledge requires duration. The instrument must be preserved through time. De vita II.1 — "Judgment requires experience. Experience is difficult because occasions are brief. Hippocrates said that art is long and is attained by along life." The epistemological argument: truth requires judgment, judgment requires experience, experience requires time, time requires preserved life. Life is afinite flame sustained by heat and moisture. Digestion renews what life consumes. Gold images incorruptibility. Planets support bodily members. Time is patterned by planetary sequence. But the stars do not compel — medicine and prudence can intervene. De vita II.20 — "Natural lifespan can be moved nearer or farther by stars and material things. Doctors and astrologers both matter." Vita naturalis propius aut longius moveri potest per stellas et res materiales. Book III opens the preserved life to the living cosmos. De vita III.1 — "Zoroaster called the correspondences of forms to World-soul reasons 'divine lures' and Synesius called them 'magical baits.'"
"Lower forms depend on celestial forms. Celestial forms proceed from reasons in the Soul. Those reasons trace back to Forms in the Intellect, and the Intellect to the One and Good."

The ladder: One, Intellect, Soul, celestial forms, lower forms, material reception. De vita III.3 — "World-soul animates all things. World-body receives form. World-spirit mediates between soul and body. Human spirit can receive world-spirit." The same architecture that structures the human being structures the cosmos. The spiritus that connects soul and body within also connects the human being to the stars. De vita III.11 — "World-soul spreads its force through quintessence as spirit inside the World's Body, especially into things that absorb more of that spirit. Things filled with purer spirit include wine, sugar, balsam, gold, precious stones, sweet smells, shining things, and hot, moist, clear, subtle substances." The same chain appears at three scales. In Book I, blood becomes spirit, and spirit serves reason. In Book II, heat and moisture sustain life, and life serves knowledge. In Book III, world-spirit flows through celestial forms into matter, and matter serves the soul'sascent. Book Irepairs. Book II preserves. Book III opens. The Apology bounds the whole within Christianity. The Magi provide the precedent: star-knowledge served revelation in the Gospel, and it can serve revelation in Ficino's Florence. De vita III.26 — "Lower things exposed to higher things. Higher gifts received through lower materials." The title De vita was bait. The reader who followed the bait through three books has been led not into forbidden knowledge but into adeeper understanding of what it means to be alive in aliving world. Titulus De vita fuit esca. Qui escam secutus est, non ad scientiam prohibitam sed ad altiorem intellectum vitae in mundo vivo pervenit. Ficino'smetaphysics asks practical questions. What should one eat? When should one study? Where should one live? What should one smell? What should one sing? What image should one contemplate? What star, place, profession, and genius belong to one'slife? The three books answer these questions in order. First, the instrument must be capable of asking them. Then it must last long enough to receive the answers. Then it must open itself to the answers that come from beyond itself. Medicine, longevity, celestial reception. Ficino needed three books because the ascent has three stages — and each stage requires the one before it. The three books of the Liber de vita form asingle ascent. Book Irepairs the instrument. Book II preserves it through time. Book III opens it to the living cosmos. Each stage depends on the one before it, and none can be skipped. Tres libri unum ascensum formant. Liber Ireparat. Liber II conservat. Liber III aperit. Quodlibet stadium apraecedente pendet. The chain that runs through all three books is the spiritus. In Book I, it is the vapour of blood that connects soul and body. In Book II, it is the substance that aging depletes and that longevity practices sustain. In Book III, it becomes the bridge between the human and the cosmic. The same architecture appears at three scales. In Book I, blood becomes spirit and spirit serves reason. In Book II, heat and moisture sustain life and life serves knowledge. In Book III, world-spirit flows through celestial forms into matter and matter serves the soul'sascent. The pattern is the same; only the scale has changed. Eadem architectura in tribus scalis apparet. In I, sanguis spiritus fit. In II, calor et humor vitam sustinent. In III, spiritus mundi per formas coelestes in materiam fluit. The Apology bounds the whole within Christianity. The Magi provide the precedent: star-knowledge served revelation in the Gospel, and it can serve revelation in Ficino's Florence. The title was bait. The reader who followed it through three books has been led not into forbidden knowledge but into adeeper understanding of what it means to be alive in aliving world. Titulus erat esca. Qui escam secutus est per tres libros, non ad scientiam prohibitam sed ad altiorem intellectum vitae in mundo vivo pervenit. The title was bait. The reader who followed it through three books has been led not into forbidden knowledge but into adeeper understanding of what it means to be alive in aliving world.
